questions.question: According to the material, your employer's own duty of care towards you means they must:
options: Give you proper training, safe equipment, clear procedures and enough support to do your job well
Remove every possible risk from the lives of the people you support
Deal with complaints without ever telling you they were made
Make every difficult decision on your behalf so you never face a dilemma

questions.question: Besides the people you support, to whom does the material say you also owe a duty of care?
options: Only to the CQC and the local authority
To your colleagues and to yourself
To no one else once you are on shift
Only to your line manager

questions.question: Your duty of care says that when a task is one you have not been trained for, you should:
options: Not attempt it, and work within your training and competence
Attempt it anyway so the person is not kept waiting
Ask the person to guide you through it themselves
Delegate it to whichever colleague is nearest

questions.question: Under the Mental Capacity Act 2005, a person who has capacity:
options: Must always follow the advice of a dietitian or GP
Is allowed to make a decision that others might see as unwise
Loses the right to choose once a professional disagrees
Can only make choices that carry no risk at all

questions.question: How does the material best describe professional boundaries?
options: Rules that only apply to managers and senior staff
Restrictions that prevent you from ever building trust
A reason to keep the people you support at an emotional distance
The lines that keep a caring relationship safe, respectful and focused on the person's needs

questions.question: A person you support tries to give you a personal gift. In line with the material, you should:
options: Accept it but keep it a secret from your manager
Politely decline it
Sell it and share the money with the team
Accept it quietly so you do not cause offence

questions.question: If you are asked to become involved in decisions about a person's money, estate or will, the material advises you to:
options: Agree, as long as the person insists it is what they want
Only get involved if no family member is available
Step back from the decision and report that you were asked
Handle it privately without troubling your manager

questions.question: According to the material, an advocate is:
options: A care worker assigned to enforce the person's care plan
A family member who takes over all of a person's choices
A senior manager who decides complaints on the person's behalf
An independent person who helps someone understand their rights and speak up for what they want

questions.question: In the material, an 'error' is defined as:
options: An event that caused or could have caused harm
A mistake, such as giving the wrong medication
Something that could have gone wrong but was caught in time
A complaint raised by a person's family

questions.question: Why does the material describe near misses as especially valuable?
options: They are early warnings that let a service fix a problem before someone is harmed
They allow the service to identify who is to blame
They rarely need to be written down or reported
They prove that a service is completely free of risk

questions.question: For your organisation, the duty of candour is described as a legal requirement under:
options: The Health and Safety at Work Act alone
CQC Regulation 20
The Equality Act 2010
The Data Protection Act

questions.question: According to the material, offering a sincere apology when something goes wrong:
options: Is not an admission of legal liability
Is only necessary if the person was physically harmed
Proves that you are a bad worker
Should be avoided until an investigation is complete

questions.question: The material suggests that behaviour which seems difficult or challenging is often:
options: A deliberate attempt to make your job harder
A reason to leave the person alone until they calm down completely
Something that should never be reported after the event
A form of communication about pain, fear, confusion or an unmet need

questions.question: If you feel a safeguarding concern is not being acted on internally, the material says you can raise it externally with:
options: The CQC or the local safeguarding team
The person's family and no one else
A colleague on the same team
The local newspaper

questions.question: Your employer also owes you a duty of care, which includes providing proper training, safe equipment and clear procedures.
options: True
False

questions.question: Meeting your duty of care means wrapping people in cotton wool and taking away their independence.
options: True
False

questions.question: You do not need to be certain that abuse is happening before you raise a concern.
options: True
False

questions.question: It is your job to fully investigate a safeguarding concern yourself before passing it on to anyone else.
options: True
False

questions.question: When someone wants to do something that carries risk, how can you support their choice safely? (Select all that apply)
options: Give them clear, honest information about the possible benefits and dangers
Overrule the choice if you personally think it is unwise
Record the decision and the risks that were discussed
Work with them, their family and the wider team to agree a plan
Refuse to discuss the risks so as not to worry them

questions.question: Which of the following are appropriate ways to handle a possible conflict of interest? (Select all that apply)
options: Keep your private phone number and social media separate from your work
Tell your manager as soon as you notice a possible conflict
Agree to be named in a person's will if they ask you
Politely decline personal gifts from the people you support
Lend money to a person you support to help them out

questions.question: After a difficult or confrontational situation, what should you always do? (Select all that apply)
options: Record what happened factually and objectively
Reflect on any triggers or warning signs so the team can prevent a repeat
Report the incident to your manager
Keep it to yourself to avoid embarrassing the person
Pretend it did not happen so that no one worries

questions.question: Which of the following are steps in working through a duty-of-care dilemma? (Select all that apply)
options: Seek support by talking to your manager rather than deciding alone
Pause to notice the competing duties or rights in the situation
Make a quick decision on your own to resolve it fast
Impose your own view of what is best for the person
Gather facts about what the person actually wants and the real risks

questions.question: An event that could have gone wrong but was caught in time and did not cause harm is called a near ___.
An independent person who helps someone understand their rights and speak up for what they want is called an ___.
The material explains that behaviour which seems difficult or challenging is often a form of ___.
